title: Microsoft Just Revealed New AI Devices (Project Solara)
transcript:
Microsoft recently invited me to Redmond to 
get an early look at Project Solara. Instead   of opening apps on your phone or computer, 
the idea is to make AI assistance available   through dedicated devices when and where you 
need it. The company showed me two prototype   AI device concepts. The first is a wearable 
badge that you can carry with you throughout   the day. The second is a desktop companion 
that sits on your desk. In this video,   I'll show you the devices, explain what 
they do, and share Microsoft's vision   for where AI-powered devices could be headed 
in the future. I'm Kevin, and let's dive in! This is the first concept device that Microsoft 
showed me. It looks a lot like an employee badge.   But instead of simply identifying who 
you are, it's designed to provide quick   access to AI throughout the day. The device 
includes a touchscreen, camera, microphones,   speaker, and a fingerprint reader. The idea 
is pretty simple. Instead of pulling out your   phone or sitting down at a computer, you 
can quickly interact with AI directly from   a device that's always with you. To make this 
more concrete, imagine you're a nurse walking   between patient rooms. Instead of writing down 
a blood pressure reading and entering it later,   you could simply use the device's camera 
to capture the information. The AI could   understand the reading and help document it 
automatically. Microsoft sees devices like this   being useful for people who spend much of their 
day away from a desk, such as healthcare workers,   retail employees, and field workers. For example, 
you could ask questions, record a conversation,   capture information using the camera, or quickly 
access information while you're on the move. Now, when I first saw this AI badge, my first 
thought was: why not just use a phone? Most of   us already have a phone with us all day. Microsoft 
says a dedicated device can provide faster access   to AI without needing to pull out your phone, 
unlock it, open an app, and then do whatever task   you're trying to accomplish. And if you're working 
in healthcare, there's another consideration.   Do you really want to use your personal phone 
to capture patient information? Probably not. The second concept that Microsoft showed me is 
designed for your desk. It stays in one place   and gives you quick access to AI throughout 
the day. The device includes a touchscreen,   microphones, speakers, and facial recognition 
for secure sign-in. Microsoft says you could   use it to review your schedule, stay on top of 
important tasks, access Microsoft 365 Copilot,   and get quick answers without constantly 
switching between apps and windows on your   PC. One interesting aspect of this device is that 
it can also work alongside a traditional computer.   Microsoft says it can connect to Windows 
365. So, if you connect it to a monitor,   you can run Windows on it through the cloud. 
Microsoft has been testing these devices   internally with employees. One piece of feedback 
that stood out to me is that some people liked   having AI available on a dedicated device instead 
of needing to open another application or browser   tab every time they wanted to interact with 
it. At a high level, you can think of this as   Microsoft's vision for what a dedicated 
AI device could look like on your desk. My biggest takeaway from Project Solara isn't 
necessarily the devices themselves. It's the   platform that Microsoft is building behind 
them. Microsoft believes AI agents will play   a much larger role in how people interact with 
technology in the future. What's interesting   is that the company isn't positioning Project 
Solara as just two devices. It's building a   platform that other hardware manufacturers and 
developers can build on top of, similar to how   Windows became the foundation for an entire 
ecosystem of PCs and applications. Of course,   it's still very early. These are concept 
devices, not products that you can go   out and buy tomorrow. But after spending 
time with the team, it became clear that   Microsoft sees Project Solara as much more than 
a badge and a desk companion. The company is   laying the foundation for what it believes could 
be the next generation of AI-powered devices. So that's a first look at Project Solara. 
Now I'm curious what you think. Would you   use a dedicated AI device like one of 
these, or would you rather keep those   capabilities on your phone and computer? 
Let me know down below in the comments.   Thanks for watching, and I'll 
see you in the next video.
